title:
Design System
in Claude Code
subtitle: An AI-first design system — shared by designers, developers, and AI.

slides.title:
Design System
in Claude Code
slides.description: Tokens, components, and rules shared by designers, developers, and AI — one source of truth across every surface.

slides.label: 01 · The Shift · From Then to Now
slides.title: From pictures of pages to design as data
slides.content: Every tool moved design closer to code — and closer to a single source of truth. That's the path that leads to building components in Storybook.
issues: Photoshop → Figma — design left the canvas for the browser
Tokens turned design into data — one source of truth
slides.highlight: Each shift pushed design and code closer. Storybook is where they meet.

image.caption: From design system to developer handoff

slides.label: 02 · System Architecture · Foundation
slides.title: Shared by designers, developers, and AI
slides.content: Tokens, a living Storybook, and every page — one set of constraints, with Claude Code as the connective layer.
issues: Storybook — the living, browsable contract for every component and state
Product pages — every screen across the web app
slides.highlight: One set of constraints — Storybook is where they become visible.

image.caption: System architecture — tokens, Storybook, pages, AI rules

slides.label: 03 · The System · Tokens, Components, Variants
slides.title: One connected library, documented in Storybook
slides.content: Tokens at the foundation, components on top, variants for every context. Storybook renders every state — the living workshop where the system is built, documented, and tested.
issues: Core components — buttons, inputs, modals, cards, tables
Variants for every state, size, and theme
Storybook — every state documented, versioned, and testable
slides.highlight: Consistency was the baseline. Extensibility was the goal. Storybook kept both honest.

slides.label: 04 · Pages & Themes
slides.title: Same component. Every page, every theme.
slides.content: One web design system. The same components compose into every page — dashboards, settings, tables, forms — while dark and light respond to the token layer.
issues: One web design system — components that compose into any page
Dashboards, settings, tables, forms — all built from the same library
Dark and light handled entirely at the token layer
Switching themes is a flag — not a rebuild
slides.highlight: One system. Every page. Every theme. Same logic underneath.

slides.label: 05 · AI as a Collaborator · Ways of Working
slides.title: AI didn't replace the work. It amplified it.
slides.content: Claude Code as a structured collaborator — explicit rules, prompts, methodologies. Designers owned intent. AI accelerated execution.
slides.highlight: AI did not replace human work. It amplified capabilities and reduced friction.

image.caption: Designer and AI — a structured collaboration

slides.label: 06 · Outcomes · What Changed
slides.title: What this project reflects about design today.

outcomes.title: Iteration
outcomes.description: Prototyping in the real stack — sprints shrink to hours.

outcomes.title: Breaking changes
outcomes.description: Shared governance catches drift before it ships.

outcomes.title: Onboarding
outcomes.description: New designers inherit the rules — the system teaches itself.

outcomes.title: The designer's role
outcomes.description: Less drawing, more defining — the system is the deliverable.

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: How my role as a designer has changed
whatWorked: Storybook became the single source of truth — design, engineering, and AI all judged work against the same rendered states, which ended the subjective 'is this on-system?' debates.
Putting tokens in code turned visual drift into a build error instead of a production bug — the system enforced itself.
My role shifted from drawing screens to defining rules. The clearer the constraints, the faster everyone else moved.
whatFailed: I assumed good work would drive adoption. It didn't — developers only reached for the system once Storybook lived inside their actual workflow, not beside it.
Early on I documented components after building them. The gap let inconsistencies slip in before the docs caught up.
whatYoudDoDifferently: Wire Storybook into the dev workflow on day one — make using the system the path of least resistance.
Lock one repeatable pattern for building a component before scaling it across the library.
Document each component in Storybook as it's built, never in a cleanup pass at the end.
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Whether the system keeps teaching itself as engineers rotate on and off. Real adoption only shows up 6+ months post-launch — beyond the window I had.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: A design system is only as good as its handoff — this is the system that made Storybook the way developers build.
